lines.text: The collector never explained the organizing principle. The dungeon has been studying the collection for decades and has identified seven possible systems, none of them entirely satisfying.
Dust and accumulated strangeness. The labyrinth's surface level holds what the rest of the archive declined to classify — which is to say, everything that refused to fit anywhere obvious.

The curiosity cabinet contains seven items that do not correspond to any known category of object, four items whose categories are known but whose presence here is unexplained, and one item the dungeon added itself.
Everything in the labyrinth is connected to everything else — not metaphorically, not philosophically, but structurally. The dungeon built the connections in when it built the shelves.

A fragment — unidentified, unclassified, unattributed. The labyrinth holds hundreds of these. They are, the dungeon believes, more honest than the identified pieces: they admit their own incompleteness.
Something is on the wrong shelf. The dungeon placed it there deliberately — either as a test or because it agreed with the misclassification. It has not clarified which.

Wood and glass and everything else. The labyrinth was built without a collection plan — it accumulated what the rest of the archive couldn't hold. The result is the most complete floor in the dungeon.
Some questions refuse categorization. They belong to every domain and none. The labyrinth was built to hold them — the questions that fell between the cracks of every organized archive.
Relevance is a temporary judgment. The dungeon does not discard based on current relevance — it has watched too many things classified irrelevant become essential a century later.
The labyrinth's last room contains the facts that were gathered after everything else was organized. The latecomers. The additions. The dungeon likes this room best.
